documentControl.purpose: This SAD describes the architecture of the Customer API Platform (CAP), Meridian Financial Services' Open Banking and partner API solution. It replaces the legacy SOAP-based Partner Integration Layer (PIL) and provides secure, high-performance RESTful APIs exposing account information and transaction data to authorised third-party providers (TPPs) and partner fintech applications.
documentControl.scope: In scope: API Gateway and all microservices (Account, Transaction, Auth, Notification); AWS infrastructure across all environments (dev, test, staging, production, DR); Integration with core banking system, fraud detection, and notification services; Security architecture including OAuth 2.0, mTLS, and encryption; Operational tooling (monitoring, alerting, logging, tracing). Out of scope: Core banking system internals (SAD APP-0102), Mobile banking application (SAD APP-0389), Partner onboarding business processes (OPS-0055), Detailed API specification (Swagger/OpenAPI on internal developer portal).

executiveSummary.solutionOverview: The Customer API Platform (CAP) is a cloud-native, microservices-based REST API platform that exposes account information and transaction data to authorised partner fintech applications and third-party providers. It is Meridian Financial Services' primary channel for Open Banking compliance and strategic partner integrations. CAP replaces the legacy SOAP-based Partner Integration Layer, which suffered from poor scalability, high latency, and an inability to meet the performance and security requirements of the UK Open Banking standard. The new platform is built on AWS using containerised microservices orchestrated by Amazon EKS, fronted by AWS API Gateway, and secured with OAuth 2.0 and mutual TLS.

businessContext.description: UK Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) mandate to provide open APIs for account information and payment initiation to authorised TPPs
The existing SOAP-based Partner Integration Layer is on unsupported middleware (Oracle SOA Suite 11g) with known security vulnerabilities
Strategic initiative to onboard 25+ fintech partners over the next 18 months, requiring a modern, scalable API platform
Current platform requires 3 FTEs for manual operational support; target is to reduce to 1 FTE with automation
Partner developers report a 4-week average onboarding time with the SOAP platform; target is under 3 days with self-service APIs

strategicAlignment.organisationStrategySupported: MFS Digital Transformation Programme (DTP-2024), specifically Workstream 3: Open Banking & Partner Ecosystem
strategicAlignment.duplicatesJustification: Replaces the legacy Partner Integration Layer (PIL) which will be decommissioned

sharedServiceReuse.justification: Used for internal admin and developer portal access
Does not support the financial-grade OAuth 2.0 profile (FAPI) required for Open Banking; using AWS API Gateway with custom authoriser
Corporate SIEM and log aggregation platform

inScope: Customer API Platform microservices: API Gateway configuration, Account Service, Transaction Service, Auth Service, Notification Service
AWS infrastructure: EKS cluster, RDS PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, S3, CloudFront, WAF, Shield
All environments: development, test, staging, production, DR
Integration with core banking (Oracle DB via Direct Connect), fraud detection (Featurespace ARIC), notification service (SES)
Operational tooling: Splunk, Grafana, PagerDuty, Jaeger
outOfScope: Core banking system modifications (separate project PROJ-0102)
Partner onboarding portal front-end (separate project PROJ-0115)
Mobile banking app integration (separate SAD APP-0389)
executiveSummary.currentState: The current Partner Integration Layer (PIL) was built in 2016 on Oracle SOA Suite 11g, hosted on-premises in MFS' Slough data centre. It provides SOAP/XML interfaces to 8 existing partner integrations. Key limitations: Performance: average response time of 1.2 seconds (P95: 3.8 seconds), far exceeding the Open Banking 1-second mandate. Scalability: vertically scaled on two physical servers; cannot handle projected 5,000 req/s demand. Security: does not support OAuth 2.0 or mTLS as required by Open Banking security profile. Supportability: Oracle SOA Suite 11g reached end-of-support in 2022; two critical CVEs remain unpatched. Cost: annual licensing and support costs of GBP 280,000 plus 3 FTEs for manual operations. Onboarding: partner onboarding requires 4 weeks of manual configuration and testing. What is being retained: Core banking Oracle database (read replicas will be consumed via new integration layer). What is being replaced: Oracle SOA Suite middleware, SOAP/XML interfaces, on-premises hosting. What is being decommissioned: PIL application servers (post 6-month parallel-run period).

keyDecisions.rationale: Corporate cloud-first strategy mandates AWS; existing enterprise agreement
Existing team skills in Kubernetes; corporate-approved platform
Relational data model for financial data; strong consistency requirements; team expertise
Decouple notification logic from core API processing; support multiple channels

compliance.regulatedActivityDetails: The platform supports PSD2-regulated account information services (AIS) provided to authorised third-party providers.

regulatoryRequirements.applicability: Mandatory -- MFS is a CMA-designated bank
regulatoryRequirements.designImpact: Must provide Open Banking APIs conforming to OBIE specifications; strong customer authentication (SCA) required

regulatoryRequirements.applicability: Applicable -- platform processes cardholder transaction data
regulatoryRequirements.designImpact: Network segmentation, encryption, access controls, audit logging, vulnerability management
Data minimisation, right to erasure support, DPIA completed, lawful basis documented

regulatoryRequirements.applicability: Applicable -- platform supports important business service
regulatoryRequirements.designImpact: RTO/RPO targets, impact tolerance testing, scenario-based resilience testing

regulatoryRequirements.applicability: MFS is certified; platform must conform
regulatoryRequirements.designImpact: Information security controls, risk assessment, access management

components.name: API Gateway
components.description: Entry point for all external API requests; handles rate limiting, request validation, API key management, and request routing

components.name: Auth Service
components.description: Handles OAuth 2.0 token issuance, mTLS validation, scope enforcement, and consent management for TPPs

components.name: Account Service
components.description: Provides account information endpoints (balances, details, standing orders, direct debits) conforming to OBIE spec

components.name: Transaction Service
components.description: Provides transaction history endpoints with filtering, pagination, and enrichment

components.name: Notification Service
components.description: Processes event-driven notifications to partners (webhooks) and internal teams (email, Slack)

components.name: PostgreSQL (Accounts DB)
components.description: Stores account metadata, consent records, and partner registration data

components.name: PostgreSQL (Transactions DB)
components.description: Stores transaction data replicated from core banking, plus API audit records

components.name: Redis Cache
components.description: Caches frequently accessed account data and rate limiting state; reduces load on core banking

components.name: Event Bus
components.description: Decouples notification and audit event processing from synchronous API flows

components.name: Audit Log Store
components.description: Long-term storage of API audit logs for compliance (7-year retention)
components.technology: Amazon S3 (Glacier Deep Archive for aged data)

components.name: Core Banking Adapter
components.description: Reads from core banking Oracle DB read replicas via JDBC; transforms data to platform domain model

designPatterns.whereApplied: AWS API Gateway fronting all services
designPatterns.rationale: Centralised rate limiting, authentication, request validation, and API versioning; decouples clients from internal service topology
Independent scaling, deployment, and failure isolation for services with different performance profiles
Decouples async processing (webhooks, emails, audit writes) from synchronous API response path; improves P95 latency
Read-optimised query model populated from core banking CDC stream; separates read path from authoritative write path in core banking

designPatterns.whereApplied: Core Banking Adapter, Fraud Detection client
designPatterns.rationale: Prevents cascade failures when downstream dependencies are degraded; implemented via Resilience4j
Gradual migration of partner traffic from SOAP to REST APIs using API Gateway routing rules
Consistent mTLS termination, observability, and traffic management across all services
Reduces latency and load on core banking for frequently accessed account data (balance lookups)

internalConnectivity.purpose: Read/write account metadata and consent records
Read transaction data and audit records
Route notification events to processing queue
Route audit events to audit processing

externalIntegrations.purpose: Read account and transaction data from read replicas
Fraud score requests for high-value transactions
Event notifications to partners (HMAC-SHA256 signed payloads)

dataView.dataSovereigntyDetails: All customer data (PII and transaction data) must remain within the United Kingdom (eu-west-2 London region). The DR region (eu-west-1 Ireland) stores only non-PII operational data (metrics, redacted logs). Cross-region replication for RDS is configured to exclude PII columns.

authorisation.entitlementStore: Okta groups mapped to Kubernetes RBAC and application roles; OAuth 2.0 token claims for external partners

useCases.name: Partner Retrieves Account Balance
useCases.mainFlow: 1. Partner sends HTTPS request with Bearer token and mTLS client certificate to API Gateway. 2. API Gateway validates request structure and routes to Auth Service. 3. Auth Service validates OAuth token, verifies mTLS certificate binding, checks consent record in PostgreSQL. 4. Auth Service returns authorisation decision to API Gateway. 5. API Gateway routes to Account Service. 6. Account Service checks Redis cache for balance (60s TTL). 7. Cache hit: return cached balance. Cache miss: Account Service queries core banking read replica via JDBC, caches result, returns balance. 8. API Gateway returns JSON response to partner. 9. Audit event emitted to EventBridge.

useCases.name: Rate Limit Exceeded
useCases.mainFlow: 1. Partner sends request to API Gateway. 2. API Gateway rate-limiting check identifies partner has exceeded 100 req/s quota. 3. API Gateway returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. 4. Rate limit event logged and counted. 5. If sustained (>5 min), Splunk alert triggers notification to Partner Manager. 6. Notification Service sends email to partner's registered technical contact.

useCases.name: Fraud Alert Triggered During Transaction Retrieval
useCases.trigger: Partner requests transaction history for an account flagged for suspected fraud
useCases.mainFlow: 1. Partner sends GET /accounts/{accountId}/transactions. 2. Request authenticated and authorised as per UC-01 flow. 3. Transaction Service queries Featurespace ARIC fraud scoring API for account risk score. 4. ARIC returns high-risk score (>0.85). 5. Transaction Service applies fraud response policy: returns limited transaction data (last 30 days only, no pending transactions), adds X-Fraud-Review: true header. 6. High-priority security event emitted to EventBridge. 7. Splunk alert fires immediately; PagerDuty pages on-call fraud analyst. 8. Notification Service sends webhook to MFS internal fraud team channel (Slack).

adrs.title: EKS over ECS for Container Orchestration
adrs.context: The platform requires a container orchestration solution to run microservices. Both Amazon EKS (managed Kubernetes) and Amazon ECS (AWS-native container service) were evaluated.
adrs.alternatives: ECS Fargate: Lower operational overhead, but limited pod-level networking control and no support for Envoy sidecar injection needed for mTLS mesh. ECS on EC2: More control but still lacks Kubernetes ecosystem (Helm, Argo CD, Calico network policies). Self-managed Kubernetes on EC2: Maximum control but unacceptable operational burden for a 6-person platform team.
adrs.consequences: Positive: rich ecosystem (Helm, Argo CD, Calico, Prometheus), strong portability to other clouds, existing team Kubernetes skills. Negative: higher operational complexity than ECS Fargate, Kubernetes version upgrade overhead every 12-14 months.

adrs.title: PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for Primary Data Store
adrs.context: The platform needs a primary data store for account metadata, transaction data, and consent records. The data is relational (accounts have transactions, consent links customers to TPPs and accounts) and requires strong consistency for financial accuracy.
adrs.alternatives: DynamoDB: Excellent scalability and operational simplicity, but poor fit for relational queries (joins across accounts/transactions/consent), no native support for field-level encryption patterns used for PII, and team has limited DynamoDB experience. Aurora PostgreSQL: Considered, but standard RDS PostgreSQL meets performance requirements at lower cost; Aurora's distributed storage overhead is unnecessary at current data volumes.
adrs.consequences: Positive: strong relational model for financial data, excellent ecosystem (pg_cron, pgcrypto for field-level encryption), team expertise, straightforward backup/recovery. Negative: vertical scaling limits (mitigated by read replicas and Redis caching), operational overhead of PostgreSQL tuning.

adrs.title: Event-Driven Architecture for Notifications and Audit
adrs.context: The platform must send notifications (partner webhooks, internal alerts, compliance emails) and write audit logs. These operations must not increase API response latency.
adrs.decision: Use Amazon EventBridge with SQS for asynchronous notification and audit processing.
adrs.alternatives: Synchronous processing: Simple but adds 50-100ms to every API response for audit writes and notification dispatch; unacceptable for P95 < 200ms target. Amazon SNS + SQS: Works but lacks EventBridge's content-based filtering and schema registry. Apache Kafka (MSK): Powerful but over-engineered for current throughput (5,000 events/s); operational overhead of Kafka cluster management not justified.
adrs.consequences: Positive: API response latency unaffected by notification/audit processing, natural decoupling enables independent scaling of Notification Service, EventBridge schema registry aids contract evolution. Negative: eventual consistency for audit logs (acceptable: audit logs are written within seconds), added infrastructure complexity.

operationalExcellence.monitoringTool: Grafana (with Prometheus data source) + Amazon CloudWatch

growthProjections.seasonalDetails: 30% traffic increase on salary payment dates (25th-28th of month), 50% increase in January (financial year activities), and 20% reduction during UK bank holidays. Auto-scaling handles these patterns.

tradeoffs.description: Multi-AZ RDS and Redis cluster mode chosen for reliability over single-AZ, incurring a 30% cost premium
tradeoffs.rationale: Tier 1 Critical platform with 99.95% SLA commitment to partners; cost premium justified by regulatory obligation and revenue impact of downtime

tradeoffs.description: EKS chosen over ECS Fargate for richer ecosystem despite higher operational complexity
tradeoffs.rationale: Kubernetes ecosystem provides superior observability, networking control (Calico, Envoy), and portability; team has existing Kubernetes skills

migration.dataMigrationMethod: CDC (Change Data Capture) from Oracle GoldenGate to PostgreSQL via Debezium + Kafka Connect
migration.dataVolume: 0 GB (no data migrated from PIL; CAP builds its own data store from core banking source)
migration.rollbackPlan: API Gateway routing rules can redirect traffic back to legacy PIL within 5 minutes; partner-specific rollback possible without affecting other partners

constraints.constraint: Must comply with PCI-DSS v4.0 for transaction data handling
constraints.impactOnDesign: Network segmentation, encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, vulnerability management, audit logging -- all mandated by PCI-DSS

constraints.constraint: All customer PII must reside within the UK (data sovereignty)
constraints.impactOnDesign: Primary region must be eu-west-2 (London); DR region (eu-west-1) restricted to non-PII data only; cross-region replication must filter PII

constraints.constraint: Must integrate with existing core banking Oracle database via read replicas
constraints.impactOnDesign: Cannot replace core banking data source; must maintain JDBC connectivity via Direct Connect; data model constrained by Oracle schema

constraints.constraint: 99.95% monthly availability SLA committed to partners
constraints.impactOnDesign: Multi-AZ deployment mandatory; active-passive DR required; auto-scaling and fault tolerance must support SLA; monthly SLA reporting to partners

assumptions.assumption: Core banking Oracle read replicas will support 15,000 queries/s at peak
assumptions.impactIfFalse: Platform cannot meet performance targets; would require caching redesign or additional read replicas
assumptions.evidence: Load test results (TEST-2025-031) confirmed 18,000 queries/s sustained

assumptions.assumption: Featurespace ARIC API will maintain <100ms P95 latency under our projected load
assumptions.impactIfFalse: Fraud checking would increase API response time beyond P95 target; circuit breaker would bypass fraud checks more frequently
assumptions.evidence: Featurespace SLA contractually commits to 100ms P95 at 10,000 req/s; no independent verification at projected 3-year volume

assumptions.assumption: Partner adoption will grow linearly to 80 partners over 3 years
assumptions.impactIfFalse: Non-linear growth could exceed capacity plans; under-adoption would mean over-provisioned infrastructure (cost waste)
assumptions.evidence: Business development pipeline shows 50 partners in negotiation; growth rate tracking to plan

risks.riskEvent: Core banking Oracle DB upgrade causes schema changes that break data replication
risks.mitigationPlan: Contract testing against core banking schema (Pact); advance notification agreement with DBA team (60-day notice for schema changes); schema compatibility layer in Core Banking Adapter

risks.riskEvent: Partner onboarding volume exceeds forecast, overwhelming support capacity
risks.mitigationPlan: Self-service partner onboarding portal (Phase 2, delivered); automated API key provisioning; partner onboarding runbook; escalation to additional support resource if queue > 5 partners

risks.riskEvent: Critical vulnerability discovered in base container image requiring emergency patching across all services
risks.mitigationPlan: Snyk continuous monitoring with P1 alert on critical CVEs; pre-built patched base images maintained in ECR; emergency deployment pipeline (bypasses staging for security patches); rollback capability

risks.riskEvent: AWS eu-west-2 region experiences prolonged outage exceeding DR activation threshold
risks.mitigationPlan: Active-passive DR in eu-west-1; quarterly DR drills; RTO 1 hour validated through testing; accept 15-minute RPO for async replication lag

dependencies.dependency: Core banking Oracle DB read replicas provisioned in eu-west-2 via Direct Connect
dependencies.evidence: Direct Connect live; read replicas operational since 2025-01-15

dependencies.dependency: Featurespace ARIC API available and contracted for CAP usage
dependencies.evidence: Contract MFS-VENDOR-2024-089 signed; API access provisioned

dependencies.dependency: Partner Onboarding Portal (APP-0456) consuming Auth Service APIs for partner registration

issues.issue: Redis cluster failover caused 45-second cache miss spike during October maintenance window
issues.resolutionPlan: Updated maintenance procedure to pre-warm cache before failover; implemented dual-write to new primary during planned failover

issues.issue: Three partners have not completed mTLS certificate renewal (certificates expiring in 60 days)
issues.resolutionPlan: Automated renewal reminders sent at 90/60/30/7 days; partner manager directly contacting non-responsive partners; contingency: temporary API key fallback (with CISO approval)

complianceTraceability.requirement: Install and maintain network security controls
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: VPC segmentation, security groups, NACLs, WAF, Shield
AES-256 encryption at rest, field-level encryption for PII, KMS key management

complianceTraceability.requirement: Protect cardholder data with strong cryptography during transmission
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: TLS 1.3 enforced for all external connections; TLS 1.2 minimum for all internal

complianceTraceability.requirement: Restrict access to system components and cardholder data by business need to know
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: RBAC + ABAC via OAuth scopes, Kubernetes RBAC, IAM least privilege

complianceTraceability.requirement: Log and monitor all access to system components and cardholder data
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: Comprehensive audit logging, Splunk SIEM integration, 7-year retention

complianceTraceability.requirement: API conformance for Account Information Services
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: REST APIs conform to OBIE specification; contract tests validate compliance

complianceTraceability.requirement: Integrity and confidentiality of personal data
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: Field-level encryption, mTLS, access controls, audit trail, DPIA completed
Consent revocation endpoint; data deletion job for expired consents; audit trail of deletions

complianceTraceability.requirement: Operational resilience for important business services
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: Multi-AZ, DR strategy, impact tolerance testing, chaos testing, quarterly DR drills

complianceTraceability.requirement: Encryption, access management, monitoring for cloud workloads
complianceTraceability.howSatisfied: KMS encryption, IAM least privilege, GuardDuty, CloudTrail, Splunk integration

glossary.definition: Adaptive, Real-time, Individual, Contextual -- Featurespace's fraud detection platform
Customer API Platform -- the solution described in this SAD
Change Data Capture -- a pattern for capturing and replicating data changes
Competition and Markets Authority -- UK regulator that mandated Open Banking
Elastic Kubernetes Service -- AWS managed Kubernetes
Financial-grade API -- an OAuth 2.0 security profile for financial services
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler -- Kubernetes auto-scaling mechanism
IAM Roles for Service Accounts -- EKS feature for pod-level IAM
Meridian Financial Services -- the fictional organisation in this example
Mutual TLS -- two-way TLS authentication where both client and server present certificates
Open Banking Implementation Entity -- the UK body governing Open Banking standards
Partner Integration Layer -- the legacy SOAP-based system being replaced
Payment Services Directive 2 -- EU directive mandating open banking
Strong Customer Authentication -- PSD2 requirement for multi-factor authentication
Third-Party Provider -- an authorised fintech that accesses bank APIs under Open Banking

references.title: OBIE Account and Transaction API Specification
references.description: Open Banking UK API specification for AIS

references.title: PCI-DSS
references.description: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard

references.title: MFS Data Classification Standard
references.description: Data classification scheme and handling requirements

references.title: CAP Threat Model
references.description: STRIDE-based threat model for the Customer API Platform

tooling.monitoring: Grafana + Amazon CloudWatch + Amazon Managed Prometheus

assessments.notes: Full version history, multiple contributors and approvers, clear scope, related documents referenced
Clear business drivers with priority, strategic alignment with reuse assessment, current-state architecture documented, business criticality justified with revenue impact
Comprehensive stakeholder register including external parties, concerns matrix fully mapped to sections, regulatory context with five applicable regulations
Full component decomposition with technology choices, design patterns documented with rationale, vendor lock-in assessed for all components, service-to-capability mapping complete
All internal and external integrations documented with protocols and authentication, API contracts versioned, end user access patterns documented, SLAs defined per interface
Deployment diagram described, compute fully specified (Graviton instances, pod sizing), full networking documented including Direct Connect, environments listed with sizing, security agents deployed
All data stores classified with retention and encryption, field-level encryption for PII, data sovereignty addressed with cross-region filtering, DPIA completed, data integrity controls evidenced
STRIDE threat model with 7 threats and mitigations, comprehensive IAM (internal + external + privileged), mTLS and OAuth 2.0 FAPI, HSM-backed encryption, SIEM integration with correlation rules
Three architecturally significant use cases crossing all views, three ADRs with alternatives and quality attribute tradeoffs
Centralised logging with Splunk, Grafana dashboards, PagerDuty alerting with escalation, Jaeger distributed tracing, comprehensive runbooks, capacity planning process
Multi-AZ with active-passive DR, RTO 1hr / RPO 15min validated through quarterly testing, chaos testing with Gremlin, fault tolerance with circuit breakers, immutable backups
P50/P95/P99 targets defined, 5,000 req/s throughput target, automated performance testing with k6, caching strategy documented, 3-year growth projections
Detailed monthly cost breakdown by component, reserved instance analysis, CloudHealth monitoring, FinOps practices documented, tagging strategy, rightsizing reviews
Graviton instances for energy efficiency, non-prod auto-shutdown, auto-scaling for demand matching. Score reduced from 5: no carbon metrics baselined, no formal sustainability KPIs.
Full CI/CD with security scanning, Strangler Fig migration plan, test strategy covering all types, weekly releases with blue-green and canary, team skills assessed, exit plan documented
4 constraints, 3 assumptions (with evidence), 4 risks with mitigation plans, 3 dependencies tracked to resolution, 2 issues tracked, compliance traceability table mapping 10 requirements
Domain-specific glossary, 9 reference documents, 6 standards/patterns referenced, full approval sign-off with JIRA references

complianceScoring.overallNotes: Comprehensive depth achieved across all sections. Exemplary documentation for a Tier 1 Critical regulated platform. Overall weighted score: 4.9/5.
